claims.text: Type 2 diabetes is a chronic and progressive cardiometabolic disorder.
The global prevalence of type 2 diabetes has reached alarming rates, with WHO estimating over 422 million adults were living with diabetes in 2014.
Type 2 diabetes is associated with a myriad of complications, including cardiovascular disease, neuropathy, nephropathy, and retinopathy.
Diabetes is a chronic disease that occurs either when the pancreas does not produce enough insulin or when the body cannot effectively use the insulin it produces.
Hyperglycaemia, also called raised blood glucose or raised blood sugar, is a common effect of uncontrolled diabetes.
Type 2 diabetes affects how your body uses sugar (glucose) for energy.
The number of people living with diabetes rose from 200 million in 1990 to 830 million in 2022.
More than half of people living with diabetes did not take medication for their diabetes in 2022.
The goals in caring for patients with diabetes mellitus are to eliminate symptoms and to prevent, or at least slow, the development of complications.
Microvascular (ie, eye and kidney disease) risk reduction is accomplished through control of glycemia and blood pressure.
Metformin is the main first-line medication for the treatment of type 2 diabetes.
Metformin is a biguanide anti-hyperglycemic agent.
Metformin works by decreasing glucose production in the liver, increasing the insulin sensitivity of body tissues.
SGLT2 inhibitors are a class of medications that inhibit sodium-glucose transport proteins in the nephron.
The foremost metabolic effect of SGLT2 inhibitors is to inhibit reabsorption of glucose in the kidney.
Apart from blood sugar control, gliflozins have been shown to provide significant cardiovascular benefit in people with type 2 diabetes.
Diabetes can affect almost every part of your body, including your heart, eyes, kidneys, and nerves.
Managing your ABCs may lower your chances of having health problems from diabetes, such as a heart attack, stroke, kidney disease, blindness, and foot or leg amputations.
Metformin is a type of medication that tells your liver to make less glucose.
Sulfonylureas tell your pancreas to make more insulin.
The American Diabetes Association updates its Standards of Care in Diabetes annually.

timeline.label: Number of people living with diabetes rose from 200 million to 830 million
Prevalence of diabetes rising more rapidly in low- and middle-income countries than high-income countries
More than half of people living with diabetes did not take medication for their diabetes
Diabetes and kidney disease due to diabetes caused over 2 million deaths
American Diabetes Association's (ADA) Standards of Care in Diabetes—2026 published
9 million people with type 1 diabetes worldwide, majority living in high-income countries
Number of people living with diabetes rose from 200 million in 1990 to 830 million in 2022
Mortality rates from diabetes have been increasing since 2000
Type 2 diabetes can be prevented with a healthy diet, regular physical activity, maintaining a normal body weight and avoiding tobacco use
Diabetes can be treated and its consequences avoided or delayed with diet, physical activity, medication and regular screening and treatment for complications
Metformin is the main first-line medication for the treatment of type 2 diabetes, particularly in people who are overweight
Metformin is generally well tolerated, but common adverse effects include diarrhea, nausea, and abdominal pain
Metformin works by decreasing glucose production in the liver, increasing insulin sensitivity of body tissues, and increasing GDF15 secretion
Metformin was first described in the scientific literature in 1922 by Emil Werner and James Bell, introduced as a medication in France in 1957
In 2023, metformin was the second most commonly prescribed medication in the United States, with more than 85 million prescriptions
The American Diabetes Association and the American College of Physicians both recommend metformin as a first-line agent for type 2 diabetes treatment
Metformin is used to lower blood glucose in those with type 2 diabetes, and has also been used to help with metabolic abnormalities in polyendocrine metabolic ovarian syndrome (PMOS)

The ADA's Standards of Care in Diabetes—2026 supports less stringent HbA1c targets for older adults with diabetes, as follows: Less than 7.0-7.5% for those with few and stable coexisting chronic illnesses and intact cognitive and functional status

A report from the ACCORD Study Group found that setting the treatment target for HbA1c below 6% in high-risk patients resulted in reduced 5-year nonfatal myocardial infarctions but nonetheless was linked to greater 5-year mortality
Review of blood glucose logs must be part of any diabetes treatment plan
SGLT2 inhibitors are a class of medications that inhibit sodium-glucose transport proteins in the nephron.
Several medications of the SGLT2 class had been approved or were under development.
The 2022 American Diabetes Association (ADA) standards of medical care in diabetes include SGLT2 inhibitors as a first line pharmacological therapy for type 2 diabetes.
The FDA issued a warning that gliflozins can increase risk of diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA).
The FDA issued a warning related to canagliflozin (Invokana) and canagliflozin/metformin (Invokamet) due to decreased bone mineral density and therefore increased risk of bone fractures.
Increased risk of lower limb amputation is associated with canagliflozin.
The A1C test shows your average blood glucose level over the last 3 months.
High blood pressure can damage your heart, kidneys, brain, and eyes.
Cholesterol is a fat, also called lipid, that is produced by your liver.
Not smoking is important for your health, especially if you have diabetes.
Medications you take in pill form are often the first option for people with type 2 diabetes.
Metformin is one of the most commonly used medications to treat type 2 diabetes.
The American Diabetes Association (ADA) updates its Standards of Care in Diabetes annually.

open_questions.text: What are the long-term effects of SGLT2 inhibitors on cardiovascular health in people with type 2 diabetes?
open_questions.why_unanswered: The compiled artifact mentions significant cardiovascular benefits, but lacks information on long-term effects.

open_questions.text: How does metformin impact glucose production in the liver and insulin sensitivity in body tissues over time?
open_questions.why_unanswered: The compiled artifact describes metformin's mechanism of action, but lacks detailed information on its long-term effects.

open_questions.text: What are the potential risks and benefits of using sulfonylureas in type 2 diabetes management?
open_questions.why_unanswered: The compiled artifact mentions sulfonylureas as a medication option, but lacks detailed information on their potential risks and benefits.

open_questions.text: How can healthcare providers effectively manage the ABCs (A1C, Blood Pressure, Cholesterol) in patients with type 2 diabetes to prevent complications?
open_questions.why_unanswered: The compiled artifact mentions managing the ABCs as a way to lower health problems from diabetes, but lacks detailed information on how to effectively manage them.

research_gaps.note: The compiled artifact relies heavily on Wikipedia and WebMD sources, which may not be as reliable or up-to-date as other sources.
The compiled artifact lacks information on the long-term effects of SGLT2 inhibitors on cardiovascular health in people with type 2 diabetes.

hypotheses.text: Type 2 Diabetes is a condition that can be treated with SGLT2 inhibitors.

observations.text: SGLT2 inhibitors are used to treat Type 2 Diabetes.

followup_queries.query: ("Type 2 Diabetes Management" AND ("SGLT2 Inhibitors" OR "Metformin") AND ("long-term effects" OR "cardiovascular health"))
followup_queries.rationale: This search query aims to find studies on the long-term effects of SGLT2 inhibitors and metformin on cardiovascular health in people with type 2 diabetes, which is a critical gap in the compiled artifact.

followup_queries.query: ("Type 2 Diabetes Management" AND ("Metformin" OR "Sulfonylureas") AND ("risks and benefits" OR "long-term effects"))
followup_queries.rationale: This search query aims to find studies on the potential risks and benefits of using metformin and sulfonylureas in type 2 diabetes management, which is a critical gap in the compiled artifact.

followup_queries.query: ("Type 2 Diabetes Management" AND ("ABCs" OR "A1C" OR "Blood Pressure" OR "Cholesterol") AND ("management" OR "complications"))
followup_queries.rationale: This search query aims to find studies on how healthcare providers can effectively manage the ABCs in patients with type 2 diabetes to prevent complications, which is a critical gap in the compiled artifact.

followup_queries.query: ("Type 2 Diabetes Management" AND ("Wikipedia" OR "WebMD") AND ("reliability" OR "up-to-dateness"))
followup_queries.rationale: This search query aims to find studies on the reliability and up-to-dateness of Wikipedia and WebMD sources, which is a critical gap in the compiled artifact.

treatments.dosing: Typical dosing: 500-1000 mg orally once or twice daily
treatments.mechanism: Decreases glucose production in the liver, increases insulin sensitivity of body tissues
treatments.name: Metformin
treatments.population: Adults with type 2 diabetes and overweight

treatments.dosing: Typical dosing: subcutaneous injection once or twice daily
treatments.mechanism: Activates GLP-1 receptors, prompting insulin secretion from pancreatic beta cells
treatments.name: GLP-1 receptor agonists
treatments.population: Adults with type 2 diabetes and obesity

treatments.mechanism: Inhibits SGLT2 proteins in the kidneys, leading to decreased glucose reabsorption
treatments.name: SGLT2 inhibitors
treatments.population: Adults with type 2 diabetes and obesity

treatments.dosing: Typical dosing: subcutaneous injection once or twice daily
treatments.mechanism: Mimics natural insulin action more closely than traditional insulin
treatments.name: Insulin analogs
treatments.population: Adults with type 2 diabetes and insulin deficiency

treatments.dosing: Typical dosing: sensor affixed to the body for continuous monitoring
treatments.mechanism: Provides real-time tracking of blood sugar levels around the clock
treatments.name: Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM)
treatments.population: Adults with type 2 diabetes and difficulty managing blood glucose levels

treatments.dosing: Typical dosing: sensor affixed to the body for continuous monitoring, insulin pump therapy
treatments.mechanism: Combines CGM with insulin pump therapy and advanced algorithms to automate insulin delivery
treatments.name: Automated insulin delivery (AID) systems
treatments.population: Adults with type 2 diabetes and difficulty managing blood glucose levels

treatments.dosing: Once or twice a day before meals
treatments.mechanism: Tells pancreas to make more insulin
treatments.name: Sulfonylureas
treatments.population: Second-line in adults with T2D and overweight

treatments.mechanism: Keeps body from breaking down hormones that give pancreas 'go' signal for insulin
treatments.name: DPP-4 inhibitors
treatments.population: Second-line in adults with T2D and overweight

treatments.name: TZDs
treatments.population: Second-line in adults with T2D and overweight

treatments.dosing: With first bite of every meal
treatments.name: Alpha-glucosidase inhibitors
treatments.population: Second-line in adults with T2D and overweight

treatments.dosing: Several pills once or twice a day with meal
treatments.mechanism: Lowers cholesterol and helps lower blood glucose
treatments.name: Bile acid sequestrants
treatments.population: Second-line in adults with T2D and overweight

treatments.mechanism: Works directly on brain to help process hormone dopamine
treatments.name: Dopamine receptor agonists
treatments.population: Second-line in adults with T2D and overweight

treatments.indication: Type 2 diabetes with chronic kidney disease
treatments.mechanism: Protects kidneys and lowers heart disease risk
treatments.name: Nonsteroidal mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists (nsMRAs)
treatments.population: Second-line in adults with T2D and overweight

evidence.name: Metformin
evidence.recommendation: Metformin is recommended as a first-line treatment for type 2 diabetes due to its strong evidence base and favorable safety profile.

guideline_timeline.body: NICE guideline CG66 on type 2 diabetes management
NICE guideline CG87 on type 2 diabetes management
WHO estimates over 422 million adults living with diabetes
IDF reports 537 million adults have diabetes, a significant rise over 6 years
WHO reports 14% of adults aged 18 and older living with diabetes, an increase from 7% in 1990
Study on metformin's effectiveness in reducing risk of developing type 2 diabetes
ADA recommends less stringent HbA1c targets for older adults with diabetes
guideline_timeline.guideline_ref: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes—2026

guideline_changes.rationale: Reducing HbA1c targets for older adults to avoid hypoglycemia and symptomatic hyperglycemia
guideline_changes.to: <7.0-7.5% for older adults with few and stable coexisting chronic illnesses and intact cognitive and functional status

guideline_changes.rationale: Adjusting HbA1c targets for older adults based on their health status
guideline_changes.to: <8.0% for older adults with significant cognitive and/or functional limitations, frailty, severe comorbidities, and a less favorable risk-to-benefit ratio of diabetes medications

treatment_conflicts.detail: One source states that SGLT2 inhibitors provide significant cardiovascular benefit, while another source indicates that TZDs have weak evidence for this effect.
One source recommends SGLT2 inhibitors as a first-line treatment, while another source suggests DPP-4 inhibitors for the same population.
One source recommends TZDs as a first-line treatment, while another source suggests DPP-4 inhibitors for the same population.
One source recommends TZDs as a first-line treatment, while another source suggests Alpha-glucosidase inhibitors for the same population.
One source recommends TZDs as a first-line treatment, while another source suggests Bile acid sequestrants for the same population.
One source recommends TZDs as a first-line treatment, while another source suggests Dopamine receptor agonists for the same population.
One source recommends TZDs for adults with type 2 diabetes and overweight, while another source suggests nsMRAs for adults with type 2 diabetes and chronic kidney disease.
